slides.title: Why Do We Follow Rules?
slides.content:
Rules help us stay safe and be kind to each other.

At school, at home, and on the street - rules are everywhere!

When we follow rules, everyone is happy and things work well.
slides.tip: Rules are not punishments - they help us live together!

slides.title: Rules at School
slides.content:
At school, we follow many important rules:

Take turns - wait for your turn to speak or play
Share - let others use your things too
Listen - pay attention when the teacher talks
Clean up - tidy your things after yourself

Take turns and clean up are phrasal verbs: a verb + a small word that changes the meaning!
slides.tip: Phrasal verbs: take turns = wait for your go, clean up = make tidy

slides.title: Rules on the Street
slides.content:
On the street, we follow traffic rules to stay safe.

We wait for the green light before crossing the road. We look left and right.

We are always polite to other people. Polite means being nice and respectful.

Being disrespectful means NOT following rules or being rude to others.

slides.title: Asking for Permission
slides.content:
It is important to ask for permission before doing something.

We say: 'May I go to the toilet?' or 'Can I borrow your pen?'

In an emergency, we must follow special rules: stay calm, listen to adults, and go to a safe place.

slides.title: Possessive Pronouns
slides.content:
Possessive pronouns tell us who something belongs to - WITHOUT using a noun after!

Whose bag is this?
It is mine. (= my bag)
It is yours. (= your bag)
It is his. (= his bag)
It is hers. (= her bag)
It is ours. (= our bag)
It is theirs. (= their bag)
slides.tip: Possessive pronouns stand ALONE. No noun after them!

slides.title: Example: Possessive Pronouns
slides.problem:
Choose the correct possessive pronoun:

1. This is my pencil. It is _____.
2. That is her book. It is _____.
3. These are our chairs. They are _____.

slides.title: Rules in Different Places
slides.html:
Different places have different rules!

In the kitchen, we don't run. We are careful with hot things. We wash our hands before cooking.

In the classroom, we raise our hand before speaking. On the playground, we share the swings.

'Whose turn is it?' asked the teacher. 'It's mine!' said Tom. 'No, it's hers!' said Sara, pointing at her friend. 'Let's take turns,' said the teacher. 'These are our rules.'

slides.title: Phrasal Verbs: Clean Up & Take Turns
slides.content:
A phrasal verb is a verb + a small word that makes a new meaning.

Clean up = make a place tidy
'Please clean up your desk after class.'

Take turns = do something one at a time
'Let's take turns on the swing.'

These are very useful in everyday English!

slides.title: Example: Phrasal Verbs in Action
slides.problem:
Fill in the blanks with clean up or take turns:

1. After lunch, we _____ the cafeteria.
2. In the game, we _____ to roll the dice.
3. Please _____ your room before bed.
steps: 1. After lunch, we CLEAN UP the cafeteria.
2. In the game, we TAKE TURNS to roll the dice.
3. Please CLEAN UP your room before bed.
slides.answer: clean up, take turns, clean up

slides.title: The Fire Drill
slides.html:
One day at school, there was an emergency drill. The alarm rang loudly!

'Stay calm!' said the teacher. 'This is ours to practise. Listen to me carefully.'

The children walked in a line. They took turns going through the door. Nobody pushed.

'Whose bag is that on the floor?' asked the teacher. 'It's mine!' said Ana. 'Leave it! In an emergency, we go quickly to a safe place.'

Everyone was polite and followed the rules. The teacher was very proud!

slides.title: Let's Review!
points: 📋 Rules help us stay safe and be kind to each other
🏫 School rules: take turns, share, listen, clean up
🚦 Traffic rules: wait for the green light, be polite
🙋 Ask permission: May I...? Can I...?
👜 Possessive pronouns: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs
🔄 Phrasal verbs: clean up (make tidy), take turns (one at a time)
